section.title: The Plague of Locusts
section.sectionRationale: The eighth plague forms its own scene, opening with God's explicit purpose that Israel will one day tell their children of this history, moving through Pharaoh's officials pressuring him, the negotiation over who may go, the devastating locusts, and another swift, broken confession.
section.thesis: God tells Moses plainly that these signs are meant to become family memory, told to children and grandchildren for generations, even as Pharaoh's officials themselves plead with him to relent and Pharaoh still tries to bargain over who gets to leave.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: God's purpose that Israel will retell this to their children; Pharaoh's officials pressuring him and the negotiation over who may go; the locusts devouring everything the hail had spared; Pharaoh's swift confession and equally swift hardening.

units.label: That you may tell your children

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses, “Go to Pharaoh, for I have hardened his heart and the hearts of his officials, that I may perform these miraculous signs of Mine among them,
and that you may tell your children and grandchildren how severely I dealt with the Egyptians when I performed miraculous signs among them, so that all of you may know that I am the LORD.”

So Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and told him, “This is what the LORD, the God of the Hebrews, says: ‘How long will you refuse to humble yourself before Me? Let My people go, so that they may worship Me.
But if you refuse to let My people go, I will bring {{arbeh|locusts}} into your territory tomorrow.
They will cover the face of the land so that no one can see it. They will devour whatever is left after the hail and eat every tree that grows in your fields.

They will fill your houses and the houses of all your officials and every Egyptian, something neither your fathers nor your grandfathers have seen since the day they came into this land.’” Then Moses turned and left Pharaoh's presence.

units.label: How long will this man be a snare?

verses.text: Pharaoh's officials asked him, “How long will this man be a snare to us? Let the people go, so that they may worship the LORD their God. Do you not yet realize that Egypt is in ruins?”
So Moses and Aaron were brought back to Pharaoh. “Go, worship the LORD your God,” he said. “But who exactly will be going?”
“We will go with our young and old,” Moses replied. “We will go with our sons and daughters, and with our flocks and herds, for we must hold a feast to the LORD.”
Then Pharaoh told them, “May the LORD be with you if I ever let you go with your little ones. Clearly you are bent on evil.
No, only the men may go and worship the LORD, since that is what you have been requesting.” And Moses and Aaron were driven from Pharaoh's presence.

units.label: Nothing green was left
units.note: the locusts devour what the hail spared

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses, “Stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt, so that the locusts may swarm over it and devour every plant in the land, everything that the hail has left behind.”
So Moses stretched out his staff over the land of Egypt, and throughout that day and night the LORD sent an east wind across the land. By morning the east wind had brought the locusts.
The locusts swarmed across the land and settled over the entire territory of Egypt. Never before had there been so many locusts, and never again will there be.

They covered the face of all the land until it was black, and they consumed all the plants on the ground and all the fruit on the trees that the hail had left behind. Nothing green was left on any tree or plant in all the land of Egypt.
Pharaoh quickly summoned Moses and Aaron and said, “I have sinned against the LORD your God and against you.
Now please forgive my sin once more and appeal to the LORD your God, that He may remove this death from me.”
So Moses left Pharaoh's presence and appealed to the LORD.
And the LORD changed the wind to a very strong west wind that carried off the locusts and blew them into the Red Sea. Not a single locust remained anywhere in Egypt.
But the LORD hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let the Israelites go.

arbeh.gloss: Locusts, one of the most feared agricultural disasters in the ancient world, capable of stripping a land bare within hours. The same word describes the devastating locust plague in Joel 1 to 2, later read as an image of the coming day of the LORD.

basic.context: This is the eighth plague. God tells Moses this history is meant to be remembered and told to children for generations. Pharaoh's own officials plead with him to relent since Egypt lies in ruins, and Pharaoh tries again to negotiate limited terms, only letting the men go. When Moses refuses, the locusts come, devouring every green thing the hail had spared. Pharaoh confesses again and asks forgiveness, but the moment relief comes, his heart hardens once more.
basic.hermeneutics: This is real history, and it explicitly tells us why: so that Israel would tell their children and grandchildren for generations what God had done. It also shows Pharaoh's negotiating tactics escalating, willing to let some go but not all, a partial obedience Scripture treats as no true obedience.
basic.originalLanguages: The plague brings arbeh, locusts, the same devastating creature described in Joel's prophecy as an image of the coming day of the LORD, capable of stripping an entire land bare within hours, exactly what happens here to everything the hail had spared.
basic.typology: The locust plague, devouring everything green, anticipates Joel's prophetic use of locusts as a picture of divine judgment on a covenant people who have strayed, later transformed in Joel 2:28-32 into a promise of restoration and outpoured Spirit, fulfilled at Pentecost, Acts 2:16-21.
basic.christ: God's stated purpose, that this history be told to children and grandchildren, anticipates the pattern of covenant remembrance culminating in the Lord's Supper, do this in remembrance of Me, Luke 22:19, where God's people continue to retell and reenact His decisive act of redemption across every generation.
basic.god: God is intentional not only about acting in history but about that history being remembered and retold, weaving remembrance into the very purpose of the plagues. He is also patient with negotiation up to a point, yet He does not accept Pharaoh's partial, self-serving compromises over who may worship Him.
basic.crossReferences: Joel 1:4 and 2:25 describe a locust plague as devastating judgment later reversed by God's mercy. Psalm 78:46 and Psalm 105:34-35 recall this plague as one of God's mighty acts. Acts 2:16-21 shows Joel's later locust-framed prophecy fulfilled in the outpouring of the Spirit.
basic.oneStory: This is another devastating strike in Egypt's judgment, deliberately remembered and retold across generations, and it exposes Pharaoh's continued pattern of partial, self-interested concessions. It anticipates how God's people are called to remember and retell His saving acts, a pattern completed in the ongoing remembrance of Christ's greater deliverance.

context.literary: The eighth plague narrative, distinguished by an extended negotiation scene involving Pharaoh's officials and the explicit question of who among Israel may go, before the locusts themselves are described devouring what the hail had spared.
context.historical: Locust swarms were among the most feared natural disasters in the ancient Near East, capable of consuming an entire season's crop within hours, a catastrophe vividly described in extrabiblical Egyptian and Near Eastern texts as well as later in Joel's prophecy.
context.canonical: Explicitly frames its own purpose as generational memory, so that you may tell your children and grandchildren, language that becomes a repeated pattern throughout Scripture for how God's people are to preserve and transmit His mighty acts, culminating in the ongoing remembrance instituted by Christ at the Last Supper.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that God's purpose in judgment includes the deliberate formation of generational memory and testimony, and to expose Pharaoh's continued pattern of negotiating partial obedience rather than full submission.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes a specific historical judgment and a specific stated purpose for its remembrance; it is not a prescription that every hardship must be retold to descendants in identical fashion, though it establishes the enduring principle that God's mighty acts are meant to be remembered and passed on.

debates.question: Was Pharaoh's offer to let only the men go a reasonable compromise or continued defiance?

views.label: Continued, calculated defiance
views.case: God's original demand consistently included the whole people, sons and daughters, flocks and herds, verse 9; limiting the release to men alone would have left the rest of Israel as hostages, ensuring their return, so this offer is read as a shrewd, insincere concession.

views.label: A partial step interpreted generously by some
views.case: Some note Pharaoh's officials themselves recognized the need to let the people go, suggesting mounting internal pressure on Pharaoh, even if his specific counteroffer remained self-serving and inadequate.

debates.bearing: Either way, Moses' outright rejection of the compromise affirms that God's call requires full, not partial, obedience, and that leaving anyone behind as leverage does not satisfy God's command.

study.originalLanguages: The arbeh, locusts, devour everything green in the land, the same devastating creature later used by Joel as an image of the coming day of the LORD, a judgment so severe that Joel later promises God will restore the years the locusts have eaten, Joel 2:25.

typology.type: The locust plague devouring the land
typology.fulfillment: Echoed in Joel's prophetic use of locusts as judgment, later transformed into a promise of restoration and the outpoured Spirit, fulfilled at Pentecost, Acts 2:16-21.

typology.type: The command to tell your children and grandchildren
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the ongoing remembrance of God's greater act of redemption instituted by Christ, do this in remembrance of Me, Luke 22:19.

study.christ: God's explicit purpose that this history be told to children and grandchildren establishes a pattern of covenant remembrance that finds its ultimate expression in Christ's instruction to His disciples, do this in remembrance of Me, Luke 22:19, ensuring that His decisive act of redemption, like the exodus before it, would be told and retold across every generation of His people.
study.god: God intentionally weaves remembrance into His redemptive acts, ensuring that what He does in history becomes testimony passed down through generations. He is patient with Pharaoh's officials pleading for relent and even with Pharaoh's own repeated attempts at partial compromise, yet He never accepts anything less than full obedience to His command.

crossReferences.note: Describes a devastating locust plague using similar language of total devastation.
God promises to restore the years the locusts have eaten, a message of hope following judgment.
Peter quotes Joel's prophecy, which draws on locust-plague imagery, as fulfilled in the outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost.
Jesus institutes the Lord's Supper as an ongoing act of remembrance, echoing the pattern established here.

study.oneStory: This is another devastating judgment on Egypt, deliberately intended to become generational memory and testimony to God's power. Pharaoh's continued partial compromises are firmly rejected, teaching that God calls for full obedience, and this pattern of remembered redemption anticipates the ongoing remembrance of Christ's far greater act of deliverance.

quiz.q: According to God's own words in verses 1 to 2, what is one purpose of these repeated signs against Egypt?

options.text: So that Israel would tell their children and grandchildren how severely God dealt with Egypt, so that all would know He is the LORD.
options.note: Verse 2 states this purpose directly, establishing generational testimony as an explicit goal of the plagues.

options.text: So that Egypt would forget the events entirely.
options.note: The opposite is stated; the events are meant to be remembered and retold, not forgotten.

options.text: So that Moses could gain personal fame.
options.note: The stated purpose centers on knowing the LORD and passing that knowledge to future generations, not personal fame for Moses.

options.text: No purpose is given for these signs.
options.note: A specific and explicit purpose is given directly in verses 1 to 2.

quiz.q: What did Pharaoh's own officials say to him before this plague fell?

options.text: They urged him to let the people go, asking if he realized Egypt was already in ruins.
options.note: Verse 7 records this direct plea from Pharaoh's own officials.

options.text: The officials had no opinion on the matter.
options.note: Their direct plea to Pharaoh is explicitly quoted in verse 7.

options.text: They encouraged him to resist Moses further.
options.note: The text records the opposite; his officials urged him to relent, not resist further.

options.text: They demanded that Moses be executed.
options.note: No such demand is recorded; their concern was for Egypt's ruined state, urging release of Israel.

quiz.q: What compromise did Pharaoh offer regarding who could leave to worship?

options.text: He made no offer at all in this passage.
options.note: His specific, limited offer is directly quoted in verses 10 to 11.

options.text: Only the men could go, not the women, children, flocks, or herds.
options.note: Verses 10 to 11 record this limited, rejected offer.

options.text: Only the elderly could go.
options.note: The text specifies men in general, not a restriction to only the elderly.

options.text: Everyone could go, with no restrictions at all.
options.note: Pharaoh specifically restricted the offer to men only, not an unconditional release.

quiz.q: What happened to the locusts after Pharaoh's confession and Moses' prayer?

options.text: A strong west wind carried them all into the Red Sea, leaving not a single locust in Egypt.
options.note: Verse 19 records this complete removal in specific, vivid detail.

options.text: They multiplied further after the prayer.
options.note: The opposite occurred; the prayer led to their complete removal.

options.text: The text does not describe what happened to the locusts.
options.note: Their removal is described in specific and vivid detail in verse 19.

options.text: They remained in Egypt indefinitely.
options.note: The text specifically records their total removal, not a lingering presence.

quiz.q: How does Joel's prophecy connect to the imagery of this plague?

options.text: Joel makes no reference to locusts anywhere in his prophecy.
options.note: Locust imagery is actually central to Joel's prophecy in chapters 1 and 2.

options.text: There is no connection between this plague and Joel's prophecy.
options.note: Joel's prophecy draws directly and extensively on locust plague imagery.

options.text: Joel uses locust imagery only to describe blessing, never judgment.
options.note: Joel's initial locust imagery describes judgment; the restoration promise comes afterward as a separate, hopeful message.

options.text: Joel uses locust devastation as an image of judgment, later promising restoration of what the locusts destroyed.
options.note: Joel 1:4 and 2:25 both draw on locust imagery for judgment and eventual restoration.

questions: God's stated purpose was that Israel would tell their children and grandchildren what He had done. What stories of God's faithfulness in your own life are you intentionally passing on to the next generation?

Pharaoh's own officials recognized Egypt was in ruins and urged him to relent, yet he still tried to negotiate limited terms. Where might you be resisting full surrender to God even while everyone around you can see the cost of your resistance?
Pharaoh offered a partial release, men only, holding back what mattered most. What are you still holding back from full obedience to God, hoping a partial commitment will be enough?

Pharaoh's confession, 'I have sinned,' sounded sincere but proved short lived once again. How do you guard your own repentance from becoming merely a reaction to consequences rather than a lasting change of heart?

Joel later transforms locust imagery from judgment into a promise that God will restore the years the locusts have eaten. What area of loss in your own life do you need to trust God can still restore?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew here uses kabed, made heavy, translated hardened, describing both Pharaoh's heart and, notably, the hearts of his officials as well, broadening the hardening beyond Pharaoh alone.
The Hebrew for Red Sea here is more literally the Sea of Reeds, yam suph, the same body of water Israel will later cross in the great deliverance of chapter 14.
